worth_it_explanation: Integral Ad Science (IAS) brand safety controls, including domain blocklists, allowlists, keyword avoidance lists, and Context Control custom contextual segments, do not enforce themselves. They require deliberate setup, regular review, and alignment with the current campaign's adjacency requirements. An IAS deployment that was scoped for one campaign and never revisited may be applying the wrong protection posture, either blocking useful inventory or leaving gaps that category-level filters alone cannot close. These checks are read-only in IAS Signal; no configuration changes are made during the audit.
intro: IAS brand safety controls fall into two layers. The first is automated suitability classification, where IAS scores content against risk tiers and the advertiser sets a floor. The second, and the focus of this subcategory, is the manual control layer: domain blocklists, domain allowlists, keyword avoidance lists, and Context Control custom contextual segments. These controls require deliberate setup and ongoing maintenance. They are configured in IAS Signal and activated in the demand-side platform (DSP) by passing IAS segment identifiers into the DSP's targeting settings. A control that exists in IAS Signal but is not activated in the DSP provides no protection. These checks confirm that the manual controls exist, that they are active in the DSP, and that they reflect the current campaign's adjacency requirements.

audit_checks.title: High-sensitivity campaigns rely on blocklists alone with no allowlist to anchor safe inventory
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A blocklist approach depends on knowing every unsafe domain or app in advance and adding it before the campaign runs. For campaigns that carry reputational risk if ads appear near harmful content, such as those for financial services, healthcare, or products marketed to families, that assumption is weak. New inventory surfaces constantly, and a blocklist can only exclude what has been explicitly added. An allowlist anchors the campaign to inventory the advertiser has affirmatively approved, so protection does not degrade as new supply emerges.

educate.base:
IAS Signal gives advertisers two approaches for domain and app-level brand safety control: blocklists and allowlists.

A blocklist identifies domains or apps the advertiser wants to exclude. When a bid request arrives from a listed domain, IAS signals the demand-side platform (DSP) to suppress the bid. The advertiser accrues protection proportional to the completeness of the list.

An allowlist takes the opposite posture. It identifies domains or apps the advertiser has affirmatively approved, and IAS instructs the DSP to bid only on that inventory. Anything outside the list is excluded by default, regardless of whether the excluded domain has been flagged as unsafe by any scoring system.

For campaigns where adjacency to harmful content carries meaningful reputational or regulatory risk, blocklist-only protection has a structural weakness: it can only exclude inventory the advertiser has already identified as unsafe. New domains, apps, and sub-domains that IAS has not yet classified, or that fall outside established risk categories, may still receive bids. An allowlist eliminates that residual exposure for the inventory types where it can be defined. The tradeoff is reach: allowlists are narrower, and the advertiser accepts a smaller available pool in exchange for higher confidence about where ads appear.

A few signs point to high-sensitivity campaigns relying on blocklists without an allowlist:
  • The IAS Signal configuration for the campaign or advertiser shows a domain or app exclusion list but no corresponding inclusion list
  • The campaign's creative or offer involves categories that carry heightened adjacency risk, such as financial products, pharmaceuticals, or content directed at children
  • No brand safety brief or campaign setup document records a deliberate decision to use blocklist-only protection
You may want to review whether the campaign's risk profile warrants the tighter allowlist posture before concluding that the blocklist-only approach is appropriate.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer, the calculation depends on the campaign's audience and creative. A broad prospecting campaign may reasonably rely on blocklists because reach matters and the brand's adjacency risk is moderate. A campaign promoting products for children or a narrowly targeted high-value segment may warrant an allowlist of approved publishers, particularly if the brand has a documented policy about placement environments. You may want to confirm which campaigns fall into each category before reviewing the control setup.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead generation advertiser, allowlists can serve a secondary function beyond brand safety. Approved publisher lists often correlate with higher-quality audiences, which can improve conversion rates alongside reducing placement risk. If the advertiser has data showing certain domains produce better lead quality, those domains are natural allowlist candidates. You may want to ask whether any placement-quality analysis has been done before concluding the blocklist approach is the right posture for every campaign.

learn_more.source_title: About Integral Ad Science's media quality verification

educate.learn_more_note: IAS Signal's blocklist and allowlist configuration interface is accessible only to advertisers with an active IAS account via the IAS Signal portal. IAS does not publish a public help center for these controls.

investigate.intro: Review the IAS Signal configuration for the campaigns in scope, then confirm how those controls are activated in the DSP.

ias_signal.label: IAS Signal
steps: Log in to IAS Signal. Navigate to the brand safety or targeting section for the advertiser.
Locate the domain and app lists configured for the campaigns in scope. Note which are exclusion lists (blocklists) and which, if any, are inclusion lists (allowlists).
For any campaign in scope that involves products or audiences carrying elevated adjacency risk, note whether an allowlist is present. Record the absence if no allowlist exists.

If the DSP for this campaign is Display and Video 360 (DV360) or another supported platform, navigate to the campaign's IAS integration settings and confirm which IAS segment identifiers are passed into the DSP. Confirm that the blocklist and allowlist segment IDs, if applicable, are activated and not just configured in IAS Signal. An IAS control that is not passed to the DSP provides no protection.
Note whether any control was last updated more than three months ago. A list that has not been reviewed since the campaign launched may not reflect current adjacency requirements.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether an allowlist exists for each campaign in scope that carries elevated adjacency risk. A campaign with an active allowlist that is passed to the DSP and reflects the current campaign context passes. A campaign where the only control is a blocklist, and where the campaign's creative or offer warrants tighter protection, is a fail recorded with the campaign name, the risk basis, and the specific gap. A campaign where blocklist-only protection is deliberate, documented, and appropriate given the campaign's risk profile is noted as acceptable.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the IAS control setup for the campaigns in scope above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more of {{site_name}}'s campaigns in Integral Ad Science (IAS) rely on domain exclusion lists alone, with no corresponding allowlist configured for placements that carry elevated adjacency risk. A blocklist can only exclude inventory already known to be unsafe, so campaigns that require higher confidence about placement environments remain exposed to new or unclassified inventory. {{voice_recommend}} evaluating which campaigns warrant an allowlist posture based on their creative content, audience, and the brand's documented adjacency requirements, then configuring and activating the appropriate allowlists in IAS Signal and confirming the segment identifiers are passed into the demand-side platform.

audit_checks.title: IAS domain or app blocklist is stale or so broad it suppresses valid inventory without review
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A blocklist that grows without review accumulates entries added for specific reasons that may no longer apply: a domain that had a brand safety incident months ago may have changed ownership or editorial standards, and a domain added because it matched a keyword pattern may have been incorrectly flagged. The result is suppressed reach without corresponding protection benefit. Conversely, a blocklist that is never expanded may miss domains that have emerged or changed since the list was last reviewed.

educate.base:
A domain or app blocklist in IAS Signal is a static list. It does not update itself when a publisher changes ownership, editorial direction, or content standards. It also does not remove entries when the original reason for exclusion no longer applies. Blocklists require periodic review to stay accurate and proportionate.

Two failure modes appear in practice. The first is a blocklist that has grown unchecked, often because entries are added quickly in response to incidents and rarely removed. A list in this state may block substantial inventory that poses no current risk, reducing reach and raising effective cost without providing additional protection. The second is a blocklist that has not been expanded since the account launched, even as new domains have emerged or existing domains have changed in ways that warrant exclusion.

IAS Signal surfaces data about where ads served and what the IAS classification scores were for those placements. That data provides a starting point for identifying whether current blocklist entries correspond to inventory that would actually pass IAS classification today, and whether domains excluded historically are still classified at a risk level that justifies the exclusion.

A few signs point to a blocklist that may be stale or disproportionate:
  • The blocklist has not been reviewed or modified in more than six months
  • The list contains several hundred or more entries, but no documentation explains the basis for individual entries
  • A significant share of blocked impressions, visible in IAS Signal reporting, comes from a small number of domains that have not been revisited
  • Recent IAS placement reports show the account serving on domains with risk scores lower than the blocklist threshold, suggesting the list is blocking inventory that IAS itself would pass
You may want to request the underlying reason for each blocklist entry from whoever manages the IAS account before recommending removals, rather than removing entries based on IAS scoring alone.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer with multiple concurrent campaigns, it is worth confirming whether a single shared blocklist is applied to all line items or whether separate lists exist for campaigns with different risk profiles. A blocklist appropriate for a family-product campaign may unnecessarily suppress reach for a campaign targeting adults with a different product. You may want to check whether the current list structure reflects the account's campaign portfolio or was built for a single campaign and inherited by all subsequent ones.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead generation advertiser, a stale blocklist may be suppressing domains that previously produced low-quality conversions but have since changed. If conversion quality data is available at the domain level, comparing it against the blocklist can identify entries that no longer correspond to underperforming inventory. You may want to ask whether the account team has access to domain-level conversion data before treating the blocklist review as a brand safety exercise alone.

educate.learn_more_note: IAS Signal's blocklist management interface, including entry timestamps and modification history, is accessible only to advertisers with an active IAS account via the IAS Signal portal. IAS does not publish a public help center for these controls.

investigate.intro: Review the current state of the domain and app blocklist in IAS Signal, including its size, recency, and whether the account has access to entry-level history.

ias_signal.label: IAS Signal
steps: Log in to IAS Signal. Navigate to the brand safety settings for the advertiser and open the domain or app exclusion list.
Note the total number of entries in the list. Note when the list was last modified, if IAS Signal surfaces that information.

Review a sample of entries, particularly those that appear generic (e.g., top-level domain patterns rather than specific pages) or that the account team cannot readily explain. Flag any entry where the basis for exclusion is unclear.

In IAS Signal reporting, check whether the blocked impressions view shows a concentration of suppressed inventory on a small number of domains. A high share of blocked impressions from a few domains may indicate the list is anchored to historical incidents rather than current risk.

Check whether any recent IAS post-campaign report flagged impressions served on domains that the blocklist was intended to cover. That would indicate the list is either not activated in the DSP or is not covering the inventory it was designed to block.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the size of the blocklist, the date of the last modification, and whether any entries could not be explained by the account team. A list that has been reviewed within the past six months, where entries correspond to a documented brand safety policy, and where the account has a process for adding and removing entries, passes. A list that has not been reviewed in more than six months, that contains entries the account team cannot explain, or that shows a high share of blocked impressions from domains whose current risk classification does not justify the exclusion, is a fail recorded with the specific findings.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the blocklist size, last modification date, and any unexplained entries above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s Integral Ad Science (IAS) domain blocklist shows signs of staleness: the list has not been reviewed recently, some entries could not be explained by the account team, or a disproportionate share of blocked impressions comes from a small number of domains whose current risk profile may not warrant continued exclusion. A blocklist that grows without review suppresses reach without a corresponding protection benefit, while one that has not been expanded may miss domains that have emerged or changed since the list was built. {{voice_recommend}} conducting a structured review of the blocklist against current IAS classification data, removing entries that no longer correspond to a documented risk basis, and establishing a review cadence, at minimum quarterly, to keep the list calibrated to the account's current adjacency requirements.

audit_checks.title: IAS keyword avoidance lists or Context Control segments are not maintained against current campaign language
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Keyword avoidance lists and Context Control custom contextual segments require the advertiser to define the terms and contexts they want to avoid. Neither mechanism updates itself as language evolves or as new topics emerge that carry adjacency risk for the brand. A list built for one campaign that is carried forward unchanged may miss risks that have emerged since and may continue blocking terms that are no longer relevant to the brand's adjacency concerns, suppressing valid inventory in the process.

educate.base:
IAS offers two controls that target content at a finer level than domain-level blocklists, reaching down to the individual page and keyword: keyword avoidance lists and Context Control custom contextual segments.

Keyword avoidance lists identify specific terms. When IAS classifies a page and finds a listed term in the content, it signals the demand-side platform (DSP) to suppress the bid for that impression. The control is fast to configure but has two limitations. First, the same word can appear in very different contexts: a keyword added to block adjacency to one topic may suppress unrelated content that uses the same term for a different purpose. Second, the list can only catch terms that have been explicitly added, so topics that emerge after the list was built are not covered.

Context Control is IAS's contextual product. Rather than matching individual keywords, Context Control evaluates the page's full content, including semantic relationships and sentiment, and classifies it against content categories and risk levels defined in IAS Signal. Advertisers can build custom contextual segments that define the content they want to avoid or, in the targeting variant, the content they want to reach. A custom segment built in IAS Signal is activated in the DSP by passing the segment identifier into the campaign's targeting settings.

Both tools require deliberate setup and periodic review. A keyword list or Context Control segment that was configured for a campaign running several months ago may not reflect the brand's current adjacency requirements, particularly if the campaign's focus has changed, if the brand has updated its brand safety brief, or if news cycles have introduced new topics that carry adjacency risk.

A few signs point to keyword avoidance lists or Context Control segments that are not maintained:
  • The keyword avoidance list in IAS Signal contains terms that were relevant to a prior campaign but do not appear in the current campaign brief
  • The keyword list has not been modified since the account was first configured
  • Context Control segments in IAS Signal were built for a specific campaign context that has since ended, and no updated segments have been created for the current campaigns
  • The DSP shows IAS segment identifiers in the campaign targeting, but those identifiers correspond to segments that IAS Signal shows as inactive or outdated
You may want to review the brand's current brand safety brief or documented adjacency requirements before assessing whether the existing keyword and segment configuration is appropriate for the campaigns now running.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer, keyword avoidance lists that were built around a single seasonal campaign may carry terms that suppress valid inventory year-round. A term added to block adjacency to content about product recalls may also match legitimate product review pages, category guides, or editorial comparisons that are appropriate placements. You may want to confirm whether keyword list terms were intended to be permanent exclusions or campaign-specific ones, and whether the current list reflects that distinction.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead generation advertiser, Context Control segments that target away from high-anxiety content categories may need to be revisited when the campaign's message or audience changes. A segment configured to avoid financial distress content for a debt consolidation advertiser may be appropriate in one campaign context and overly restrictive in another. You may want to review the current segment configuration against the active campaign brief before concluding whether the existing segments are still calibrated correctly.

learn_more.source_title: About Integral Ad Science's media quality verification

educate.learn_more_note: IAS Signal's keyword avoidance list interface and Context Control segment builder are accessible only to advertisers with an active IAS account via the IAS Signal portal. IAS does not publish a public help center for the configuration of these controls.

investigate.intro: Review the keyword avoidance lists and Context Control segments configured in IAS Signal for the campaigns in scope, and confirm they reflect the current campaign and brand adjacency requirements.

ias_signal.label: IAS Signal
steps: Log in to IAS Signal. Navigate to the brand safety or contextual controls section for the advertiser.

Locate any keyword avoidance lists configured for the advertiser or for specific campaigns. Note when each list was last modified. Review a sample of terms against the current campaign brief to confirm they correspond to active adjacency concerns.

Locate any Context Control custom segments configured in IAS Signal. For each segment, note the content categories or contexts it is configured to avoid or target, and confirm whether the segment's definition aligns with the current campaign's adjacency requirements.

For each active IAS segment or keyword list, check the DSP campaign settings to confirm the corresponding segment identifier is activated. A segment configured in IAS Signal but not passed to the DSP as an active targeting or exclusion parameter provides no protection.

Note any segments that IAS Signal shows as inactive, expired, or associated with ended campaigns, and confirm whether those segments' identifiers are still referenced in the DSP. Identifiers that remain in DSP settings after the corresponding IAS segment has been deactivated may produce unexpected behavior.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the keyword avoidance lists and Context Control segments active in IAS Signal, when each was last modified, and whether each has been confirmed active in the DSP. A configuration where keyword lists and segments reflect the current campaign brief, have been reviewed within the past quarter, and are confirmed active in the DSP passes. A configuration where lists were last modified before the current campaign launched, where terms or segment definitions do not match the active campaign context, or where IAS segment identifiers in the DSP do not correspond to active IAS Signal segments is a fail recorded with the specific gaps.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the keyword avoidance list and Context Control segment configuration from IAS Signal above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s Integral Ad Science (IAS) keyword avoidance lists or Context Control custom contextual segments have not been maintained against the current campaign's adjacency requirements. Keyword lists and contextual segments do not update themselves as language evolves, news cycles shift, or campaigns change, so a configuration built for a prior campaign may be suppressing valid inventory, missing current risks, or referencing IAS segment identifiers that are no longer active in IAS Signal. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing all keyword lists and Context Control segments against the active campaign brief and the brand's current brand safety documentation, updating or retiring entries that no longer correspond to current adjacency concerns, and establishing a review process tied to each new campaign launch so controls are calibrated before campaigns begin serving rather than carried forward unchanged.
